---
title: "How the Backward Elimination Step Works in the `mars` Package"
author: "Shivansh Ghai"
format:
  html: default
editor_options:
  markdown:
    wrap: 72
---

## 1. Purpose

This note explains how `bwd_stepwise()` implements the backward pruning
stage of the project MARS package. The forward step creates a large basis
expansion, and the backward step reduces it to a smaller model that
balances fit and complexity.

So the point of the backward step is not merely to delete terms. It is to
choose a subset of basis functions that gives a good penalized fit.

## 2. Main idea

The input to `bwd_stepwise()` is the output of `fwd_stepwise()`, which
contains:

- `y`: the response vector
- `B`: the forward basis matrix
- `Bfuncs`: the symbolic basis-function definitions

At each stage, the backward algorithm asks:

> If I delete one basis function from the current model, which deletion
> gives the best penalized fit?

It tries every one-at-a-time deletion, keeps the deletion with the lowest
lack-of-fit score, and continues. It also remembers the best model seen
anywhere along the pruning path.

## 3. The lack-of-fit criterion `LOF()`

The package uses:

```r
LOF <- function(formula, data, control) {
  fit <- lm(formula, data = data)
  rss <- sum(stats::residuals(fit)^2)
  M <- ncol(data) - 1L
  C_M <- M + control$d * (M - 1L) / 2
  rss / nrow(data) / (1 - C_M / nrow(data))^2
}
```

This combines:

- a fit term through RSS
- a complexity penalty through `C_M`

Here:

- `M` is the number of basis columns in the candidate model
- `d` is the penalty parameter from `mars.control()`
- `C_M = M + d(M - 1)/2`

As the model gets larger, the penalty grows. So the backward step is not
just minimizing RSS; it is looking for a smaller and better-balanced
model.

## 4. Initialization

The function begins with:

```r
Mmax <- ncol(fwd$B)
Jstar <- 2:Mmax
Kstar <- Jstar
dat <- data.frame(y = fwd$y, fwd$B[, c(1, Jstar), drop = FALSE])
lofstar <- LOF(y ~ . - 1, dat, control)
```

The first column of `fwd$B` is `B0`, the constant basis function, so it is
always kept. The code therefore tracks only the non-constant basis
indices.

The key objects are:

- `Kstar`: the best subset at the current stage
- `Jstar`: the best subset seen anywhere in the whole pruning path
- `lofstar`: the best LOF value seen so far

This distinction matters because the best overall model may appear before
the end of the pruning sequence.

## 5. Outer loop: shrink the model gradually

The outer loop is:

```r
for (M in Mmax:2)
```

So the algorithm starts with the full forward model and repeatedly
considers smaller models.

At each stage it sets:

```r
b <- Inf
L <- Kstar
```

where:

- `L` is the current active set of non-constant basis indices
- `b` stores the best stagewise LOF among the one-deletion candidates

If tracing is enabled, the active set is printed with:

```r
if (control$trace) cat("L:", L, "\n")
```

## 6. Inner loop: test each possible deletion

For each basis index `m` in the active set, the code forms a reduced
subset:

```r
K <- setdiff(L, m)
dat <- data.frame(y = fwd$y, fwd$B[, c(1, K), drop = FALSE])
lof <- LOF(y ~ . - 1, dat, control)
```

This means:

1. remove one basis term
2. keep the intercept basis `B0`
3. fit the reduced model
4. compute its LOF score

This is the core backward elimination step in MARS.

## 7. Choosing the best stagewise deletion

The best deletion at the current stage is tracked using:

```r
if (lof < b) {
  b <- lof
  Kstar <- K
}
```

So `Kstar` becomes the reduced subset with the smallest LOF among all
one-deletion candidates at that stage.

That subset is then used as the starting point for the next outer-loop
iteration.

## 8. Tracking the best model overall

The code separately updates the best overall subset with:

```r
if (lof < lofstar) {
  lofstar <- lof
  Jstar <- K
}
```

So even if a model is not just the best stagewise deletion but the best
model seen anywhere on the pruning path, it is remembered in `Jstar`.

At the end, the returned pruned model is based on `Jstar`, not simply on
the final `Kstar`.

## 9. Small conceptual example

Suppose the forward pass produced basis columns:

- `B0`
- `B1`
- `B2`
- `B3`
- `B4`

The backward step begins by testing all one-term deletions:

- keep `{B2, B3, B4}`
- keep `{B1, B3, B4}`
- keep `{B1, B2, B4}`
- keep `{B1, B2, B3}`

If the LOF scores are:

| kept subset | LOF |
|---|---:|
| `{B2, B3, B4}` | 0.42 |
| `{B1, B3, B4}` | 0.36 |
| `{B1, B2, B4}` | 0.39 |
| `{B1, B2, B3}` | 0.48 |

then the best stagewise deletion is the one leaving `{B1, B3, B4}`.
That subset becomes the next working model, and if `0.36` is also the
lowest LOF seen so far, it becomes `Jstar` as well.

## 10. Keeping the intercept basis

At the end of the procedure, the intercept basis is restored explicitly:

```r
Jstar <- c(1, Jstar)
list(y = fwd$y, B = fwd$B[, Jstar, drop = FALSE], Bfuncs = fwd$Bfuncs[Jstar])
```

This ensures that the final basis matrix includes `B0`, which acts as the
intercept in the later linear model fit.

That matches the fitting step in `mars()`, which uses:

```r
lm(y ~ . - 1, data = data.frame(y = y, bwd$B))
```

The `- 1` is used because the basis matrix already contains the constant
term.

## 11. Why this implementation is reasonable

This backward pruning approach works well for an educational MARS package
because:

- it is easy to understand
- it follows the standard “grow then prune” MARS strategy
- it keeps the symbolic basis definitions and numerical basis matrix
  aligned
- it returns a pruned model that can still be used for interpretation and
  prediction

Its main simplification is computational cost: each pruning stage refits
multiple linear models from scratch. That is acceptable here because the
goal is clarity rather than production-level optimization.

## 12. Summary

`bwd_stepwise()` simplifies the forward basis expansion by repeatedly:

1. trying all one-term deletions
2. computing the penalized LOF score for each reduced model
3. keeping the best stagewise deletion
4. remembering the best overall subset seen during pruning

So the backward step turns the large forward model into a smaller final
model by balancing goodness of fit against complexity.
